name: Bhaviveka
dates_notes: Bhāviveka is conventionally placed c. 500–570 CE on the basis of his polemical engagement with the early Yogācāra (post-Vasubandhu, pre-Dharmapāla) and the absence of references to Dharmakīrti, with Candrakīrti's reply placing him before c. 600. Eckel (Bhāviveka and His Buddhist Opponents, 2008) defends the sixth-century date.

lineage_polemical.note: Candrakīrti's Prasanna-Padā opening is the principal Prāsaṅgika rebuttal of Bhāviveka's svatantra-anumāna methodology; the Prāsaṅgika / Svātantrika distinction is generated by the Bhāviveka–Candrakīrti exchange.

The Tarkajvālā contains an extended refutation of the Yogācāra vijñapti-mātratā position attributed to Asaṅga's Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha and the Maitreya-corpus, on the grounds that the trisvabhāva analysis covertly reintroduces svabhāva at the paratantra level and so cannot stand as a Mahāyāna alternative to Mādhyamaka.

Bhāviveka's Yogācāra-refutation in the Madhyamaka-Hṛdaya engages Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā specifically: the position that nothing exists apart from vijñapti is rejected as a Mahāyāna form of the Sarvāstivāda dharma-realism it claims to surpass.

core_thesis: Bhāviveka founds *Svātantrika Mādhyamaka*: the position that *svatantra-anumāna* (independent positive inference) is admissible at the conventional level without compromising Nāgārjuna's ultimate *śūnyatā*. The motivating concern is methodological: a Mādhyamaka that confines itself to *prasaṅga* (consequence-refutation) cannot demonstrate its own positive theses to opponents who reject the tacit shared ground required for *prasaṅga* to bite. Bhāviveka's *Madhyamaka-Hṛdaya-Kārikā* with the *Tarkajvālā* auto-commentary provides positive *anumāna*-style demonstrations of *śūnyatā* itself, framed in Dignāga-influenced inferential idiom — *vyāpti*, *pakṣa-dharmatā*, *udāharaṇa* — while preserving the ultimate refusal of *svabhāva*. The text is also the principal Mādhyamaka anti-Yogācāra polemic: the *trisvabhāva* analysis is rejected as covertly reintroducing *svabhāva* at the *paratantra* level, and *vijñapti-mātratā* is shown to substitute a cognition-realism for the external-object realism it had refuted. Bhāviveka's commentary on the MMK, the *Prajñā-Pradīpa*, develops the Svātantrika reading of Nāgārjuna and is the immediate target of Candrakīrti's *Prasanna-Padā*.

engaged_works.title: Tarkajvala
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the auto-commentary on Bhāviveka's own Madhyamaka-Hṛdaya-Kārikā. Survives complete in Tibetan; partial Sanskrit fragments.

ascription_evidence.description: Eckel 2008; the Tibetan transmission preserves both base text and commentary as a unit.

engaged_works.summary: Bhāviveka's auto-commentary on his *Madhyamaka-Hṛdaya-Kārikā* ("Heart of the Middle Way"), an eleven-chapter doxographic-and-polemical treatise. The first three chapters supply the positive Mādhyamaka, structured as Dignāga-influenced *anumāna*s for *śūnyatā* — Bhāviveka's distinctive Svātantrika contribution. Chapters 4–8 are a systematic refutation of rival schools — *śrāvaka-yāna*, Yogācāra, Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Vedānta, Mīmāṃsā — each treated with extensive *pūrva-pakṣa* presentation followed by Mādhyamaka rebuttal. Chapter 4 (against the *Śrāvaka-yāna*) and chapter 5 (against Yogācāra) are the philosophical centre: the Yogācāra-refutation engages the *trisvabhāva* doctrine and the *vijñapti-mātratā* thesis on the grounds that they reintroduce *svabhāva* in disguise. Chapter 8 (against Vedānta) is a unique source: it preserves a sixth-century non-dualist Vedāntic position (the *aupaniṣadika*-pakṣa) refuted from the Mādhyamaka side, a position pre-dating Śaṅkara and important for the historical reconstruction of pre-Śaṅkara Advaita.

engaged_works.title: Prajna-Pradipa
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the MMK; survives complete in Tibetan with Avalokitavrata's sub-commentary the Prajñā-Pradīpa-Ṭīkā.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard attribution in both Tibetan and Chinese transmission.

engaged_works.summary: Bhāviveka's chapter-by-chapter commentary on Nāgārjuna's MMK, supplying the Svātantrika reading: each MMK chapter is rendered as a sequence of *svatantra-anumāna*s with explicit *vyāpti*-statements and *udāharaṇa*-illustrations, demonstrating the Mādhyamaka conclusion in inferential form. The commentary is the immediate target of Candrakīrti's *Prasanna-Padā*: Candrakīrti's opening rebuttal of *svatantra-anumāna* is structurally a refutation of Bhāviveka's commentarial method, not a refutation of any particular Bhāviveka thesis. The Tibetan tradition treats the *Prajñā-Pradīpa* as one of the three principal Mādhyamaka commentaries on the MMK alongside Buddhapālita's *Vṛtti* and Candrakīrti's *Prasanna-Padā*; the Prāsaṅgika–Svātantrika distinction crystallises as a doxographic category through the relation of these three commentaries.

reception_notes: Bhāviveka's reception in the Indian Vedāntic tradition is largely indirect: he is the targeted Mādhyamaka in Vedāntic refutations only as one member of the school. The Tarkajvālā chapter on Vedānta is significant in the other direction — as a sixth-century Buddhist witness to a pre-Śaṅkara non-dualist Vedāntic position whose existence is otherwise hard to attest.
